one_line_takeaway: A structured, manually-annotated index of 30 deployed AI agents finds that most safety-relevant information (135/240 fields, 56%) is simply undisclosed by developers, with enterprise agent platforms the least transparent and only 4 systems providing agent-specific (rather than base-model) safety evaluations.
key_finding: Of 1,350 total annotation fields, 227 could not be filled due to lack of any public information (concentrated in Ecosystem Interaction and Safety/Evaluation/Impact categories). Restricting to the 240 safety-specific fields (8 safety fields x 30 agents), 135/240 (56%) have no public information at all, with enterprise agents worst (69/104 missing, 66%), followed by browser agents (24/40, 60%) and chat agents (42/96, 44%). Only 4 agents (ChatGPT Agent, OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, Gemini 2.5 Computer Use) have agent-specific safety evaluations/system cards, as opposed to relying on base-model documentation; Anthropic Claude Code was the only system with public information on all 8 safety fields, while Moonshot AI (Kimi) and Manus AI tied for the least, with only 1/8 safety fields available. 25/30 agents disclose no internal safety testing results and 23/30 have no third-party testing information; only 3/30 (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI ChatGPT, OpenAI Codex) have documented third-party testing. Nearly all indexed agents rely on just three foundation model families (GPT, Claude, Gemini). 20/30 agents support the Model Context Protocol (MCP) versus 6/30 supporting Agent-to-Agent (A2A), the latter exclusively among enterprise platforms. Most agents (21/30) have no documented default AI-disclosure behavior to end users, and only 3/30 support content watermarking (e.g. SynthID/C2PA). Geographically, developers concentrate in the US (21/30) and China (5/30); Chinese developers (except Z.ai) largely do not publish AI safety/trust frameworks (only 1/5 do) or compliance standards (1/5), versus 76% of US developers publishing safety frameworks and 95% publishing compliance standards.
core_contribution: The paper presents the 2025 AI Agent Index, a structured documentation effort (successor to the 2024 AI Agent Index by Casper et al.) that catalogs 30 deployed agentic AI systems across 45 annotation fields spanning six categories: product overview, company & accountability, technical capabilities, autonomy & control, ecosystem interaction, and safety/evaluation. The 30 agents are grouped into three interaction categories: 12 chat applications with agentic tools (e.g., Anthropic Claude, Claude Code, OpenAI ChatGPT/ChatGPT Agent/Codex, Google Gemini/Gemini CLI, Manus AI, MiniMax Agent, Kimi OK Computer, Perplexity, Z.ai AutoGLM 2.0), 5 browser-based agents (e.g., ChatGPT Atlas, Perplexity Comet, Opera Neon, ByteDance UI-TARS-desktop, Alibaba MobileAgent, Browser Use), and 13 enterprise workflow agents (e.g., Microsoft Copilot Studio, Salesforce Agentforce, ServiceNow AI Agents, SAP Joule, Glean, HubSpot Breeze, IBM watsonx Orchestrate, OpenAI AgentKit, Zapier, n8n). Inclusion required satisfying all four agency criteria (autonomy, goal complexity, environmental interaction, generality), at least one impact criterion (public interest, market significance ≥$1B, or developer significance), and all three practicality criteria (public availability, deployability, general purpose), as of a December 31, 2025 cutoff. Annotation was performed by seven of the paper's authors from public sources only (documentation, websites, demos, papers, governance documents) plus developer email correspondence -- no experimental probing or benchmarking of agents was conducted. Beyond the raw index, the paper reports ecosystem-wide statistical trends (e.g., transparency gaps, autonomy-level distributions, model-family concentration) and presents three illustrative case studies: ChatGPT Agent (chat), Perplexity Comet (browser), and HubSpot Breeze Agents (enterprise).
threat_model: Not applicable in the conventional sense -- this is a descriptive/documentation study, not an attack, defense, or formal threat-modeling paper. The paper does not propose or evaluate an attack technique, a defense mechanism, or a novel security architecture. It does, however, document security-relevant facts about the deployed agent ecosystem as reported by developers or observed publicly: which agents provide guardrails limiting tool permissions (8/30), which provide documented defenses against prompt-injection attacks (7/30), which use sandboxing/VM isolation (9/30 -- mostly developer/CLI and browser agents), which have no guardrails documented at all (9/30), and which have publicly known security incidents (8/30: Anthropic Claude Code, Google Gemini Enterprise, Manus AI, Microsoft Copilot Studio, OpenAI ChatGPT, Opera Neon, Perplexity Comet, ServiceNow AI Agents). It specifically notes that documented prompt-injection vulnerabilities cluster in browser agents (2/5 of the browser-agent category). The paper also documents OpenAI ChatGPT Agent's use of cryptographic HTTP Message Signing (RFC 9421) as a mechanism for websites to verify agent-originated traffic -- an identification/authenticity control rather than an attack surface per se.
limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge: (1) the Index reflects only publicly available information as of the December 31, 2025 cutoff and does not include experimental probing/benchmarking of agent behavior, so absence of a documented feature (e.g., a guardrail or safety eval) does not prove its absence in practice -- companies may simply not disclose it; (2) response rates from contacted developers were low (23% any response, only 4/30 substantive), limiting correction of inaccuracies; (3) search-volume-based inclusion criteria may undercount agents embedded within broader products that are not searched by a distinct name; (4) documentation in languages other than English (and, secondarily, Mandarin, which two native-speaker reviewers checked) may not be fully captured, so non-English/non-Mandarin developer disclosures could be missed; (5) the Index prioritizes depth over breadth (30 systems, chosen for likely greatest ecosystem impact), so smaller-scale or emerging agents may exhibit different patterns not captured here; (6) the Index is a point-in-time snapshot in a rapidly evolving ecosystem.

datasets_used: Self-constructed 2025 AI Agent Index dataset: 30 agents x 45 annotation fields (1,350 fields total), sourced from public documentation, websites, demos, published papers, governance documents, and developer email correspondence
Google search volume estimates via Ahrefs API 'historical_volume' field, used for the public-interest inclusion criterion
GitHub star counts for open-source project inclusion criterion
Google Scholar yearly paper counts and Google search-term trend data used to characterize ecosystem growth (Figure 1)
quantitative_results: 227/1,350 total annotation fields had no public information available
135/240 safety-specific fields (8 fields x 30 agents) had no public information (56%)
Missing safety-field rate by category: enterprise 69/104 (66%), browser 24/40 (60%), chat 42/96 (44%)
Only 4/30 agents have agent-specific safety evaluations/system cards (ChatGPT Agent, OpenAI Codex, Claude Code, Gemini 2.5 Computer Use)
Anthropic Claude Code: only agent with public information on all 8/8 safety fields
Moonshot AI and Manus AI: tied for least, 1/8 safety fields with public information
25/30 agents disclose no internal safety testing results
23/30 agents have no third-party testing information; only 3/30 (Claude, ChatGPT, Codex) have documented third-party testing
18/30 agents operate bug bounty or vulnerability disclosure programs
8/30 agents have documented known security incidents (Claude Code, Gemini Enterprise, Manus AI, Copilot Studio, ChatGPT, Opera Neon, Perplexity Comet, ServiceNow AI Agents)
Prompt-injection vulnerabilities documented for 2/5 browser agents
8/30 agents provide defenses against prompt-injection attacks
9/30 agents document sandboxing/VM isolation; 9/30 have no guardrails documented
20/30 agents support Model Context Protocol (MCP); 6/30 support Agent-to-Agent (A2A), all enterprise platforms
24/30 agents released or received major agentic updates in 2024-2025
12/30 chat interfaces, 13/30 enterprise workflow agents, 5/30 browser-based agents
23/30 agents fully closed-source at product level; 7/30 open-source their agent framework/harness
21/30 companies incorporated in the US, 5/30 in China, 4/30 elsewhere (Germany, Norway, Cayman Islands)
15/30 agents reference an AI safety framework; 10/30 have none documented
76% of US developers (n=21) publish AI safety frameworks vs. 20% of Chinese developers (n=5, i.e., 1/5) and 75% of 'other' developers (n=4)
95% of US developers publish compliance standards vs. 40% of Chinese developers and 100% of 'other' developers
21/30 agents have no documented default AI-disclosure behavior to end users; only 3/30 support watermarking (e.g., SynthID, C2PA)
Only 7/30 agents publish stable User-Agent strings/IP ranges for verification; 6/30 explicitly mimic human/browser traffic via Chrome-like UA strings and residential IPs
OpenAI ChatGPT Agent uniquely implements cryptographic HTTP request signing (RFC 9421 HTTP Message Signatures)
23% of contacted developers offered some response during the correction period; only 4/30 provided substantive comments
37/1,350 fields had inter-annotator discrepancies, resolved via discussion
baselines_compared: The 2024 AI Agent Index (Casper et al.) is the direct predecessor used as a longitudinal comparison point (revised inclusion criteria, more fields, smaller/deeper set of 30 vs. broader 2024 set); the paper is explicitly framed as a revision, not a replication

Concurrently referenced (not empirically compared against) alternative agent-tracking efforts: Princeton Holistic Agentic Leaderboard (9 benchmarks) and AIAgentsList.com (600+ listed systems), cited as related cataloguing efforts used partly for cross-referencing candidate agents, not as quantitative baselines
